questions.question: What should dictate your CRM choice?

options.text: The CRM with the most features
Your actual sales motion — the way you acquire customers
The most popular CRM on G2 reviews

questions.explanation: Your sales motion — the way you actually acquire customers — should dictate your CRM, not the other way around. A $14/month tool that matches your motion beats a $200/month tool that doesn't.

questions.question: For a B2B volume motion (100-500+ outreach touches per week), what is a CRM must-have?

options.text: Built-in sequences or tight integration with outreach tools

questions.explanation: B2B volume motions need built-in sequences or tight integration with outreach tools, bulk actions, activity logging, pipeline velocity metrics, and potentially calling/SMS. Close, HubSpot Starter, and Pipedrive are best fits.

questions.question: What was Sarah's mistake with HubSpot?

options.text: She didn't spend enough on it
She built an enterprise sales machine for a coaching business that closed 2-3 clients per month through warm conversations
She didn't integrate it with her email

questions.explanation: Sarah spent 3 months configuring HubSpot with custom fields, workflows, and integrations for an enterprise sales model. But her coaching business closed 2-3 clients per month through warm conversations — she needed a simpler, relationship-focused tool.

questions.question: There is a single 'best CRM' that works for all solo founders regardless of sales motion.
questions.explanation: False. There is no 'best CRM.' There's only the best CRM for how you actually sell. A B2B volume seller needs sequences and bulk actions. A coach needs relationship tracking. A creator needs audience segmentation. Match the tool to the motion.

questions.question: Describe your sales motion (B2B volume, relationship-based, creator, or hybrid). Based on that motion, which CRM features are must-haves vs nice-to-haves? Which CRM tool best fits your specific motion and why?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response clearly identifies their sales motion pattern, distinguishes must-have CRM features from nice-to-haves based on that motion, and selects a specific CRM with reasoning tied to their actual sales behavior rather than features or popularity.
